title: Friend in Trouble
introduction: Casey gets this message from what appears to be a friend's account. Tap anything suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text: hey its me!!! my phone died and im using a friend's phone. im stranded at the shopping centre and my parents arent answering. can you send me €30 via revolut so i can get a taxi home? ill pay you back tomorrow i promise
script.flag_label: Urgency + money request from unexpected account
script.explanation: This is a 'friend in need' scam — one of the most emotionally effective. The account may be hacked or spoofed. The urgency ('stranded', 'parents not answering') is designed to make you act before you think. The request is always money, always urgent, always a reason they can't use their own funds.

script.text: I've been trying to reach mum and dad but they're not picking up either.
Emma? Is this really you? Which shopping centre?
yes its me!! im at the one near school you know the one. please hurry im really scared i just need €30 its not a lot please
script.explanation: A real friend would know which shopping centre. Vague answers to specific questions — 'the one near school' — indicate the sender doesn't actually know Casey. The emotional escalation ('really scared', 'please') is pressure to stop asking questions and just send money.

script.text: I know it's a lot to ask, I wouldn't if I wasn't desperate.
I'm going to call your actual number to check.
no i told you my phone is dead!! just send the money please i need to get home. why don't you trust me??
script.explanation: The scammer can't let Casey call Emma's real number — that would immediately reveal the fraud. Blocking verification while applying guilt ('why don't you trust me?') is the clearest possible signal this isn't Emma. Real friends in trouble welcome verification.

script.text: I texted Emma's mum. She says Emma is at home right now.

learning_objectives: Always verify an unexpected money request through a different channel
Vague answers to specific questions are a major red flag
Anyone blocking verification is almost certainly not who they claim to be
Emotional pressure ('I'm scared', 'don't you trust me') is a manipulation tactic
The correct response is: verify first, send money never without confirmation
debrief:
This is an account hijacking scam. Emma's account was hacked — or the message came from a fake account — and the scammer is using her identity to exploit Casey's trust.

**Casey did everything right:** asked a specific question, tried to call, contacted a family member. That's exactly the playbook.

**The rule for money requests from friends:** Always verify through a completely separate channel — call their actual number, contact a family member, check in person. If they're genuinely in trouble, a 2-minute verification call won't hurt. If they resist verification, it's a scam.
